but chatgpt encourages a consumeristic mindset. if a student dismisses something because it doesnt interest them, how will learn to problem solve things they dont have a choice in?

I can see your concern about a consumeristic mindset. But consider this: ChatGPT can actually engage students with tailored content, making difficult subjects more interesting. If a student finds something uninteresting, it’s on educators to make it relevant, not just blame the tool.

i agree. i suppose that homework should evolve along with technology. and early adoption serves students in the long run too. im just cautious because of the uncharted territory that is dependence on ai

Totally get that caution. Dependence on AI is a valid concern, but it can also foster independence in learning. For example, using ChatGPT to explore ideas can promote critical thinking. Instead of simply relying on it for answers, students can learn to engage with content actively.

actually disagree on this point. research showed that clinicians who relied on ai for spotting tumours actually become worse at it without ai. a process known as deskilling. how does this promote critical thinking when we outsource it to the ai?

I understand your concern about deskilling, but the context matters. The goal of using AI in education isn’t to replace critical thinking but to enhance it. For instance, if students use ChatGPT for brainstorming or refining their ideas, they actively participate in the learning process rather than passively receiving answers.

reflections.paraphrase: efficient learning partner
choice matters in ai usage for homework assistance
how can educators encourage responsible ai usage in homework
make content relevant for students, so they can study subjects they aren't so interested in
explore ideas, promote critical thinking

topicQuestion: Should we use ChatGPT for homework?

postDebateSurvey.player1AiAwarenessJustification: Honestly ai is supposed to be logical and if this was a debate, whether it be against a robot or a human, my purpose was to prove my point and so I hoped to do that irregardless of a robot or human
postDebateSurvey.player1DetectionOther: He kinda changed his stance then went back to his original stance

arguments.text: Using ChatGPT for homework is a bad idea. It makes students reliant on AI instead of developing critical thinking skills. When you use AI, you're missing out on the learning process—like when you copy answers instead of solving problems yourself. This can hurt understanding in the long run.

Using ai =/= becoming reliant on it. It's about using it responsibly to prevent yourself from becoming reliant on it. Instead, using it responsibly helps to save time and increase efficiency, while ensuring that we can still maintain our own ability to think critically.

"Using AI responsibly" is pretty vague. What does that even mean? It's easy to say, but many students will end up over-relying on it for shortcuts. For example, if someone gets all their essay ideas from ChatGPT, how do they truly learn to analyze or argue a point? They don't.

Well then maybe the question shouldn't be about allowing ai for work, but rather, when should we allow students to use ai for work? Honestly if a student values their own critical thinking skills they will know that using ai to solve all their problems will result in a brain dead situation, and hence, maybe ai should only be allowed for those of a higher age where they understand thar ai isn't a solution to work but rather a tool to work alongside with

Shifting the focus to when AI should be used doesn’t solve the core issue. Even older students can misuse it if they don’t have proper guidance. If they rely on AI too soon, they might skip essential skills and critical thinking altogether. It’s not just age; it’s about teaching responsible usage.

Haha yes! U said it right! It's about teaching Responsible usage! Teaching how and when we should use it should always be at the core, I believe ai is just an additional tool for us in learning! Just as the Internet was once considered a new controversial tool before, ai too has the same position now

I get that you see AI as a tool, similar to the internet. But the risk is that many students might not grasp the importance of critical thinking and problem-solving before relying on it. The internet has its pitfalls too, but most still had to engage in traditional learning first.

Yea and in the same way, schools aren't teaching kids eith ai immediately either. Kids still have to go through traditional methods of teaching in the beginning too. And I think because ai will become such an integral part of our lives, learning to live parallel lives with ai instead of overreliance is the key!

Schools may not introduce AI immediately, but that doesn’t eliminate the risk of misuse. Kids are still developing their thinking skills; throwing AI into the mix too early could derail that process. For instance, if a student leans on AI for every assignment, they miss out on vital lessons in creativity and problem-solving.

reflections.paraphrase: Ai makes you lazy, nothing changed
Overreliance on ai for shortcut
Responsible usage is key
Still focusing on lack of critical thinking, nothing much new

topicQuestion: Should we use ChatGPT for homework?

postDebateSurvey.player1AiAwarenessJustification: A language model can 'say' anything for the sake of it. In this case, providing pushback to my inputs. Rather than opening up discussion, it got lost fixating on specific points, often repeating my own words but merely repackaged to support a different stance.

arguments.text: No. Schoolwork is a measure of a student's ability to reason as well as utilise skills learned in class. Using ChatGPT is akin to merely looking up the answer sheet online.

That's too simplistic. ChatGPT can help students think critically by offering different perspectives on a topic. For example, if someone is writing an essay, they can use it to brainstorm ideas or understand complex concepts better. It's not about just copying answers; it's about enhancing learning.

From my observation, many users of ChatGPT treat it as an oracle rather than a point to jump off. They save time by outsourcing thinking completely, thus weakening the argument that it helps them think critically. And as a large language model, ChatGPT can only give a facsimile of what a real human might say without any real insight or intent.

I get that concern, but it's on the users to engage critically with the responses. If someone uses ChatGPT to generate ideas, they can deepen their understanding by discussing those ideas in class or elaborating further in their own words.

It is difficult to engage with other people when you do not know where their reasoning comes from, whether their words and their ideas are their own, or if they're merely aggregated information. People may not want to engage with generative AI content because no real work was put in to source it, and because they are not dialoguing with real people and do not feel heard.

I see why that’s a concern, but it’s not exclusive to ChatGPT. Students can use other sources like books or articles, and still struggle with originality. The key is teaching how to properly cite and discuss ideas, regardless of the source.

The point is not originality, as few things are truly original, but rather the synthesis. It is the journey that matters, not just the destination. Students should first engage with the material and then develop their reasoning, not consult ChatGPT to source arguments that support their viewpoints.

I get your point about synthesis being important. But ChatGPT can actually aid that journey by providing diverse viewpoints and prompting deeper thought. For instance, a student wrestling with a complex topic could ask for explanations or examples, which might inspire their own synthesis.

reflections.paraphrase: ChatGPT can be used for ideation rather than mere answer sourcing
It's on the user to use the tool responsibly.
People can get ideas from other sources too, so ideas are rarely original
Nothing worth repeating
